questions.question: Define primitive data structures and list three examples. Explain briefly why they are considered primitive.
keyPoints: Primitive data structures are basic data types directly operated upon by machine instructions
Examples include integer, float, and character
They are considered primitive because they are fundamental building blocks not composed of other data types

questions.question: Explain the characteristics of primitive data structures, including their predefined nature, simplicity, direct manipulation, and fixed size.
keyPoints: Predefined: Built into the programming language
Simple: Represent single values
Direct manipulation: Can be directly operated upon by machine instructions
Fixed size: Typically have a fixed memory size

questions.question: Compare and contrast linear and non-linear non-primitive data structures. Provide two examples of each and discuss the advantages and disadvantages of one example from each category.
keyPoints: Linear structures arrange data sequentially (Array, Linked List)
Non-linear structures allow complex relationships (Tree, Graph)
Advantages of Arrays: Fast access using index, simple implementation
Disadvantages of Arrays: Fixed size, insertion and deletion are expensive
Advantages of Trees: Efficient searching, hierarchical data representation
Disadvantages of Trees: Complex implementation, may have unbalanced trees

questions.question: Explain how the classification of data structures (primitive and non-primitive) forms a hierarchy. Describe the relationship between these classifications and how non-primitive structures are built upon primitive ones.
keyPoints: Hierarchy begins with primitive types (int, char) as the foundation
Non-primitive structures are constructed by organizing primitives into complex arrangements
A node in a linked list contains a primitive data field and a primitive pointer/reference
Non-primitive further classified into linear and non-linear
Diagram showing the complete classification hierarchy

questions.question: Discuss the importance of choosing the right data structure for a given problem. Explain how factors such as type of operations, frequency of operations, memory constraints, access patterns, and time complexity requirements influence this choice.
keyPoints: Importance of choosing the right data structure: Efficiency, scalability, and maintainability
Factors influencing the choice: Type of operations, frequency of operations, memory constraints, access patterns, and time complexity requirements
Examples of how these factors influence the choice of data structure
Trade-offs between different data structures
